retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Targeting and I Cell Diseases -- Cellular Level -- Clathrin
retrieved_docs.content: Clathrin-coated vesicles are essential for lysosome biogenesis, endocytosis, retrograde transport (endosome-to-TGN), and anterograde transport (TGN-to-endosomes). [19] [26] Their sizes vary from 50 to 80 nm. The structure of clathrin is described as a 'triskelion' due to the combination of 3 heavy chains that each associate with a light chain being approximately 190 kDa and 25 kDa, respectively. Having a pH below 7.0 promotes clathrin cage formation. [27] Clathrin-coated vesicles contain high amounts of MPR (mannose-6-phosphate receptors). Clathrin-coated vesicles almost entirely populate the TGN, with COPI vesicles also present to a lesser extent. Clathrin adaptor proteins can be either monomeric or tetrameric. Clathrin adaptor protein 1 (AP1), a heterotetrameric protein, functions to form vesicles at the TGN, sort lysosomal hydrolases, and retrieve cargo like SNAREs, MPRs, sortilin, and furin. [10] AP2, which forms at the cell membrane, mediates the clathrin-dependent endocytosis. AP3 may directly transport proteins from the TGN to lysosomes, but the function of AP4 has not been clearly defined. [28] The externalized LDL (low-density lipoprotein) receptor takes LDL into the cell via clathrin-mediated endocytosis. Once LDL binds to its receptor, a clathrin-coated pit forms, which intracellularly moves the LDL receptor-ligand complex. Clathrin dissociates from these vesicles and then returns to the plasma membrane for further use. [29] [30] Dynamin, a GTPase, plays a role in the scission of clathrin-coated vesicles. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Phagocytosis -- Issues of Concern -- Receptor-led Formation of the Phagosome
retrieved_docs.content: As the phagosome traverses in the direction of the phagocyte’s centrosome, it fuses with a lysosome containing degradative enzymes such as proteases and iron-sequestering molecules such as lactoferrin. This fused organelle is termed the phagolysosome and gradually acidifies to activate proteolytic and degradative enzymes. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Targeting and I Cell Diseases -- Molecular Level -- Transport of Lysosomal Proteins (Enzymes)
retrieved_docs.content: Lysosomal integral membrane protein type 2 (LIMP-2/SCARB2) is the MPR-independent trafficking receptor responsible for beta-glucocerebrosidase transport. [40] The pH-dependent cargo (beta-glucocerebrosidase) is bound to LIMP-2 in the RER. The complex travels across the cis- Golgi to the trans -Golgi and is taken to the lysosome, where the acidic pH disconnects the complex. [40] LIMP types I, III,10, 12, and 39 also cross the Golgi in an anterograde fashion. The lack of involvement of beta-glucocerebrosidase in the M6P-dependent trafficking pathway accounts for its normal serum levels in ICD. The identification that LIMPs can transport to lysosomes in the presence of tunicamycin, a drug that blocks the traditional transport of hydrolases, further adds evidence that LIMP transport is not coupled with that of soluble hydrolases and to the fact of the existence of an MPR-independent pathway. [41] Cathepsin D, alpha-glucosidase, and acid phosphatase are other enzymes in this less-used delivery system. [42] Sortilin is another receptor that can bind several targets, such as neurotensin and receptor-associated proteins, via an M6P-independent route. It can perform Golgi to lysosome transport of prosaposin, GM2 activator protein, acid sphingomyelinase, cathepsin D, and cathepsin H. [31] Though some are detailed, the exact mechanisms of how each lysosomal enzyme is delivered via the M6P-independent trafficking pathway are yet to be fully explored. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The earlier the onset, the more severe the dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The study of this organelle has paved the way for understanding how LSDs affect different organs and the methods to investigate and treat them.

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The degradation function of lysosomes has three components. Among them, phagocytosis and endocytosis concern extracellular particles. The former leads to the formation of a phagosome and the latter an endosome. The process of degrading endogenous substances is called autophagy. It results in an autophagosome that is then extruded out of the cell by exocytosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The substrates accumulate within cells because of a lack of a specific enzyme and cause organ dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Catabolism -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Once inside a lysosome, lysosomal proteases called cathepsins can degrade cytosolic proteins labeled for catabolism. Cathepsins are another type of cysteine protease present within pericellular environments, cytosol nuclei, and mitochondria. [15] Tumor necrosis factor signaling, cell stress, and caspase can trigger the lysosomal release of cathepsin, cleaving intracellular proteins like Bid and leading to downstream activation of the apoptotic caspase cascade. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing is also helpful in detecting carriers and helps in family counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A patient clinically suspected of having an LSD, but with normal enzyme levels and no mutation in the enzyme coding gene should be further investigated. Such patients can have a mutation in the gene for the enzyme activator. A protein called prosaposin (PSAP) has four subunits A, B, C, and D. A gene codes for prosaposin. The code for each subunit occupies separate points on the gene. The prosaposin protein is cleaved into its subunit components. Each subunit functions as an activator protein for a specific enzyme. In the absence of its activator, the normal enzyme is rendered functionless. The substrate accumulates and may leak into the urine; examples are MLD (saposin B) and Gaucher disease (saposin C). [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Apart from the recycling of substrates, lysosomes are involved in other crucial functions to cellular homeostasis. Lysosomes have three major components: 1. Luminal proteins which are either acid hydrolases (enzymes) or their activators. 2. Membrane-integral proteins: Structural proteins(amino acid and lipid transporters). Ion-channels like calcium channels. Trafficking and fusion machinery. [9] Membrane catabolic enzymes. Vesicular ATPase which functions both as a proton pump and nutrient sensor. 3. Lysosomal associated proteins. Abnormalities in one or more of the above components can disrupt lysosomal functions and result in LSDs. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: There are specific clinical characteristics for the individual LSDs that provide the lead to the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: A low enzyme level with appropriate clinical suspicion and the normal gene encoding the enzyme should prompt a search for mutations in the genes for enzyme activator proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Phospholipase A2 -- Molecular Level -- Lysosomal PLA
retrieved_docs.content: Lysosomal PLA derives its name due to its studied localization to lysosomes within cells. It contains the characteristic catalytic amino acid triad consisting of aspartic acid, serine, and histidine that allows it to function as a phospholipase with acyl-transferase activity and specificity for the substrates phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) and phosphatidylcholine (PC) within lysosomes. The enzyme can function independently without direct interaction with calcium. However, its activity can be altered by the presence of calcium that interacts with other upstream or downstream compounds that regulate its activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: They are rare individually, some very rare, but they are much more common as a group.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastric Intrinsic Factor -- Mechanism -- Ileal Events
retrieved_docs.content: After its recognition by cubilin, the IF-Cbl complex gets endocytosed into the enterocyte bound to the cubam complex. Inside the endosome, the IF-Cbl complex gets liberated, and cubam gets recycled to the apical membrane. Within the lysosome, Cbl is released from IF by the action of lysosomal cathepsin L. [23] Cbl will be exported from the lysosome to the cytoplasm via a transporter called LMBD1. [24] The transport of Cbl to the bloodstream is mediated by ABC transporter multidrug resistance protein 1 (MRP1) located in the basolateral membrane of ileal cells. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2490
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is an antiretroviral medication that functions as an inhibitory proteas, meaning it acts as a protease inhibitor that blocks the activity of viral proteases essential for HIV replication, highlighting its therapeutic role in managing HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is connected to an entity called inhibitory proteas, which is classified as ixazomib; thus, the inhibitory proteas component associated with amprenavir is identified as the proteasome‑inhibiting drug ixazomib.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2594
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas as a broader category that includes more specific proteasome‑inhibiting agents. The entity inhibitory proteas is identified as ixazomib, indicating that ixazomib is a particular proteasome inhibitor falling under this category. Additionally, remikiren is classified as a type of inhibitory proteas, meaning that remikiren also belongs to the same class of proteasome‑inhibiting compounds associated with ixazomib. Together, amprenavir, inhibitory proteas, ixazomib, and remikiren illustrate a hierarchical relationship among proteasome inhibitors, with amprenavir encompassing the broader class, inhibitory proteas representing the specific class, and ixazomib and remikiren being individual agents within that class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1096
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, encompass several specific agents including ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. All of these drugs share the common mechanism of inhibiting viral proteases, a broader relationship described as “inhibitory proteas.” Within this class, amprenavir is explicitly linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, underscoring its role in protease inhibition. The collective activity of these protease inhibitors as direct‑acting antivirals is central to antiviral treatment strategies, particularly in the management of HIV infection, where blocking viral protease function is essential for suppressing viral replication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the antiviral agents ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir and tipranavir. Within this class, amprenavir is linked to a subcategory termed inhibitory proteas, and the broader protease‑inhibitor class is also associated with the inhibitory proteas concept. Inhibitory proteas is identified as the proteasome‑inhibiting drug ixazomib, and it encompasses the specific agent remikiren. These hierarchical and functional connections illustrate how antiviral protease inhibitors relate to proteasome‑targeting compounds, underscoring shared mechanistic themes that are important for drug classification, therapeutic decision‑making and the development of treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1982
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir, hirudiny, ixazomib, and remikiren are all connected through the concept of inhibitory proteas (proteasome inhibition). Amprenavir has a child entity identified as inhibitory proteas, indicating that a proteasome‑inhibiting component is derived from or associated with amprenavir. Hirudiny is classified as an inhibitory proteas, meaning it functions as a proteasome inhibitor. The inhibitory proteas class is linked to ixazomib, a clinically used proteasome inhibitor, suggesting that inhibitory proteas share mechanistic or structural characteristics with ixazomib. Additionally, remikiren is positioned as a subordinate entity within the inhibitory proteas hierarchy, indicating that remikiren is a specific proteasome‑inhibiting agent related to the broader inhibitory proteas category. Together, these relationships illustrate how various drugs and compounds are organized around the central pharmacologic theme of proteasome inhibition, which is relevant for therapeutic strategies targeting protein degradation pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 85
retrieved_docs.content: (proteasome inhibitor) category, indicating potential effects on proteasome activity. Within this hierarchy, amprenavir is a child of the inhibitory proteas subgroup and is contraindicated when co‑administered with cisapride monohydrate; cisapride monohydrate is itself contraindicated with furosemide and with the triazole antifungal flukonazol. The inhibitory proteas class also contains hirudiny, classified as a type of inhibitory proteas and positioned as a subclass of the proteasome inhibitor ixazomib, illustrating a nested relationship among proteasome‑targeting agents. Systemic antimycotics are organized into distinct ATC classes: the “Other antimycotics for systemic use” class comprises kaspofungin and micafungin, with kaspofungin derived from a lipopeptidy parent scaffold that is also shared by the antiviral bulevirtide, indicating structural similarity; the “Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics” class includes itraconazol, flukonazol and vorikonazol. Understanding the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1091
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors that are direct‑acting antivirals comprise the agents fosamprenavir, amprenavir and tipranavir. Amprenavir is linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, indicating that it functions as a proteasome‑inhibiting compound. The broader class of protease inhibitors, direct‑acting antivirals, also has a relationship with inhibitory proteas, suggesting that members of this class commonly exhibit proteasome‑inhibitory activity. In addition, hirudiny is classified as an inhibitory proteas, and remikiren is identified as an inhibitory proteas as well. Together, these connections underscore the role of various antiviral protease inhibitors and related molecules in targeting proteasomal pathways, which is relevant for therapeutic strategies aimed at viral replication and proteasome‑mediated processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 477
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors that are classified as direct‑acting antivirals form a therapeutic group that includes fosamprenavir, amprenavir, indinavir and tipranavir. Within this group, amprenavir is linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, indicating that it possesses or is associated with proteasome‑inhibitory activity. More generally, protease inhibitors as a class have a broader relationship with inhibitory proteas, suggesting overlapping mechanisms between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition. The inhibitory proteas category itself comprises agents such as ixazomib and the compound hirudiny, both of which act as proteasome inhibitors. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians understand how antiviral protease inhibitors may intersect with proteasome‑targeting drugs, informing decisions about combination therapy, potential cross‑reactivity, and management of adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 679
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral agents that target viral proteases. Key members of this class include the protease inhibitors ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. Amprenavir is linked to a downstream entity termed inhibitory proteas, reflecting a broader relationship between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition. Inhibitory proteas is classified as ixazomib, a proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. This connection highlights the pharmacologic overlap where agents originally developed as viral protease inhibitors share mechanistic features with proteasome‑targeting drugs, informing both antiviral therapy and oncology drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1936
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors that function as direct‑acting antivirals comprise a pharmacologic group that includes ritonavir, tipranavir, amprenavir and atazanavir. These agents are linked not only by their ability to inhibit viral proteases but also by a broader association with proteasome inhibition pathways. Amprenavir, in particular, is connected to a downstream entity described as “inhibitory proteas,” indicating that it may give rise to or be related to proteasome‑inhibiting activity. The “inhibitory proteas” concept itself is classified under the proteasome inhibitor ixazomib, and the peptide‑derived compound hirudiny is identified as an instance of this inhibitory proteas class. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which several antiviral protease inhibitors intersect with proteasome‑targeting mechanisms, suggesting potential overlapping therapeutic effects or shared molecular targets relevant to antiviral and anticancer drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 402
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a therapeutic class that includes the specific agents fosamprenavir, atazanavir and tipranavir, each of which is a member of this antiviral protease‑inhibiting group. Beyond their antiviral activity, protease inhibitors share a broader pharmacologic relationship with compounds that act as inhibitory proteas, linking viral protease inhibition to proteasome inhibition pathways. Within the inhibitory proteas category, both hirudiny and ixazomib function as proteasome‑inhibiting agents, illustrating that they are types of inhibitory proteas. Remikiren is associated with the inhibitory proteas class through an inverse relationship, suggesting that it is not a direct member but rather a molecule whose activity is counter‑regulated or targeted by proteasome inhibition. This network of relationships underscores the overlap between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition, providing insight into shared mechanisms that may influence drug selection, combination therapy, and potential repurposing strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 653
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals and neuraminidase inhibitors, direct acting antivirals are both subclasses of direct acting antivirals. Rimantadine is a member of the cyclic amine subclass and produces the physiologic effect known as Cellular Synthetic Activity Alteration, indicating that it alters cellular synthetic activity. Zanamivir is a member of the neuraminidase inhibitor subclass, placing it within the broader category of direct acting antivirals. These connections show how individual antiviral agents such as rimantadine and zanamivir are organized within specific antiviral classes and highlight rimantadine’s mechanism of altering cellular synthetic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 384
retrieved_docs.content: complications in infected patients. Racecadotril is described as an enzyme inhibitor and is mapped from the related compound thiorphan, illustrating structural and functional relationships among enzyme‑targeting drugs. Collectively, these entities demonstrate how dermatologic agents, cardiovascular medications, enzyme‑inhibiting compounds, and repurposed drugs intersect within clinical pharmacology and emerging therapeutic strategies, particularly in the context of COVID‑19.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitory proteas refers to a class of proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Ixazomib is a specific drug that belongs to this inhibitory proteas class, acting as a reversible proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. Remikiren, although primarily recognized as a renin inhibitor, is also categorized within the inhibitory proteas group because it exhibits proteasome‑inhibitory activity, placing it alongside ixazomib as another member of this therapeutic class. This relationship highlights that both ixazomib and remikiren share a common mechanism of proteasome inhibition, despite their differing primary indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 358
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors that function as direct‑acting antivirals comprise fosamprenavir, amprenavir, indinavir and tipranavir, linking these agents to the broader class of protease‑targeted antiviral therapy. Amprenavir is specifically associated with an “inhibitory proteas” entity, indicating that it may possess proteasome‑inhibitory properties, and the entire class of protease inhibitors, direct‑acting antivirals, is described as having a broader relationship with inhibitory proteas, suggesting overlap between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition mechanisms. Hirudiny is identified as an instance of inhibitory proteas, reinforcing its role as a proteasome‑inhibiting compound. The inhibitory proteas concept is further classified as a type of ixazomib, placing ixazomib within the proteasome‑inhibitor family, and it is also noted as a subclass of remikiren, indicating that remikiren represents a broader category that encompasses these proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Together, these connections illustrate how antiviral protease inhibitors and proteasome inhibitors intersect, which can inform therapeutic strategies that target both viral replication and protein degradation pathways.
